claim: Agent-first UX design: ask whether all product features are accessible to non-human agents (APIs, MCPs, tools), not just browser UI
actionable: Audit all features: are they accessible via APIs or MCPs? If not, build them; agents are now first-class users
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Proactive agents > reactive chat agents; background agents triggered by events save far more time than user-prompted agents
actionable: Design agents to run continuously in the background (SRE monitoring, security scanning) rather than waiting for user prompts
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Evals are the biggest mistake to skip early; vague agents consume development time on unmeasured tweaks; clear evals enable rapid iteration
actionable: Before building a second agent, write offline evals with representative test cases; measure before optimizing
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Three-tier evals: offline (representative data set), online (real production data), living (continuously updated from customer interactions)
actionable: Start with offline evals, add online monitoring, then set up pipelines to pull real user traces back into offline test sets
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Durable agents (e.g., via Temporal) handle timeouts, retries, and failures in cloud environments; raw agents fail silently in production
actionable: Use a durability framework (Temporal, etc.) for background agents; do not rely on raw LLM calls for long-running tasks
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Bitter lesson of agents: general methods (better models + tools) beat clever handcrafted solutions; handcrafted optimizations regress with new models
actionable: Invest in strong tools and evals rather than prompt engineering tricks; be ready to tear out customizations when new models arrive
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Stay model agnostic: models leapfrog each other monthly; good evals let you swap models instantly without rewriting infrastructure
actionable: Build evals that test independent of model; when Claude/GPT/Llama update, re-run evals and route to best model without code changes
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Framework adoption has doubled; use frameworks (LangGraph, Pydantic, LangChain) to avoid reinventing durability, logging, routing patterns
actionable: Select a framework that handles durability and observability; don't build agent orchestration from scratch
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Memory across agents enables learning from user interactions; extract semantic knowledge from traces and prune stale context over time
actionable: Maintain shared memory layer (semantic knowledge base) across agents; use agents to clean up stale context automatically
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Multiplayer agent systems: human-human collaboration + human-agent (annotation, feedback) + agent-agent (shared enclaves, network policies)
actionable: Design agent teams with human feedback loops (annotation, correction); isolate agent-to-agent communication in secure enclaves
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents

claim: Reinforcement learning on agent traces is coming; log all tool calls, context, and outcomes for future RL training
actionable: Set up comprehensive logging of agent interactions today; structure logs to enable RL training in the future
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Diamond Bishop: The Next 100 Agents
